title: At the Chemist
summary: A customer visits a chemist to buy medicine for a cold. The pharmacist asks about her symptoms, recommends suitable products, and gives advice on dosage and rest.

body.speaker: Pharmacist
body.text: Good afternoon. How can I help you today?

body.speaker: Customer
body.text: Hi, I've had a bad cold for a couple of days and I was hoping you could recommend something for it.

body.speaker: Pharmacist
body.text: Of course. Can you tell me what your main symptoms are?

body.speaker: Customer
body.text: I have a runny nose, a sore throat, and I feel quite tired. I also had a mild fever last night, but it seems better today.

body.speaker: Pharmacist
body.text: I see. Have you taken anything for it so far?

body.speaker: Customer
body.text: Just some paracetamol for the fever. I haven't tried anything else.

body.speaker: Pharmacist
body.text: That was a good idea. For the runny nose and congestion I'd recommend a decongestant nasal spray. It works quite quickly.

body.speaker: Customer
body.text: Is it safe to use? I'm not taking any other medication at the moment.

body.speaker: Pharmacist
body.text: Yes, it's perfectly safe. You should use it no more than three times a day and not for more than seven days in a row.

body.speaker: Customer
body.text: Alright. And what about my sore throat? It's quite painful in the mornings.

body.speaker: Pharmacist
body.text: I'd suggest these throat lozenges. They have a mild anaesthetic in them so they help to numb the pain. Suck one every three to four hours.

body.speaker: Customer
body.text: Perfect. Are there any side effects I should know about?

body.speaker: Pharmacist
body.text: They can sometimes cause a slight numbing of the mouth, but that's normal. Just don't eat or drink for thirty minutes afterwards.

body.speaker: Customer
body.text: That's good to know. How much are these altogether?

body.speaker: Pharmacist
body.text: The nasal spray is six ninety-nine and the lozenges are three forty-nine, so ten forty-eight in total. And make sure you get plenty of rest — it really does help.

body.speaker: Customer
body.text: Thank you so much. I'll take both. I'll definitely try to rest more.

annotations.exampleEn: She had a runny nose and kept sneezing all morning.
The doctor said he had a mild fever of 37.8 degrees.

annotations.meaningVi: paracetamol (thuốc hạ sốt và giảm đau)
annotations.exampleEn: Take two paracetamol tablets every four to six hours for the pain.
The pharmacist recommended a decongestant spray for her blocked nose.
Use the nasal spray twice a day for no longer than a week.
She bought a pack of throat lozenges to soothe her sore throat.
The dentist applied a local anaesthetic before the procedure.
The cold weather numbed his fingers.
Some common side effects of this medicine include drowsiness and nausea.
The doctor advised her to get plenty of rest and drink lots of water.

grammarNotes.title: Present perfect for recent experience: 'I've had a cold', 'I haven't tried anything'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Hiện tại hoàn thành (have/has + past participle) được dùng khi nói về một trạng thái hoặc kinh nghiệm bắt đầu trong quá khứ gần và vẫn còn liên quan đến hiện tại. 'I've had a bad cold for a couple of days.' — Tôi bị cảm lạnh được vài ngày rồi (và vẫn đang bị). 'I haven't tried anything else.' — Tôi chưa thử gì khác. 'Have you taken anything for it so far?' — Bạn đã dùng gì cho nó chưa? Từ 'so far' (cho đến nay) là dấu hiệu của thì hiện tại hoàn thành.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The present perfect (have/has + past participle) connects a past action to the present moment. Key signal words in this dialogue: 'for a couple of days' (duration), 'so far' (up to now), 'haven't tried' (negative experience so far). 'I've had a cold for two days' = it started two days ago and I still have it now. Compare with simple past: 'I had a cold last month' (finished, no longer relevant). Pharmacists and doctors commonly use this tense: 'Have you taken any medication?'

grammarNotes.title: Modal verbs for advice and instruction: 'should', 'I'd recommend', 'I'd suggest'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Should + động từ nguyên thể' dùng để đưa ra lời khuyên. 'You should use it no more than three times a day.' — Bạn nên dùng không quá ba lần một ngày. 'I'd recommend / I'd suggest' là những cách lịch sự hơn để đề nghị điều gì đó. 'I'd recommend a decongestant nasal spray.' — Tôi khuyên dùng thuốc xịt mũi thông mũi. Chúng thường được dùng khi người có kiến thức chuyên môn (như dược sĩ, bác sĩ) đưa ra lời khuyên.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Three structures for giving advice in this dialogue: (1) 'should + infinitive' — 'You should use it no more than three times a day.' (2) 'I'd recommend + noun/gerund' — 'I'd recommend a decongestant nasal spray.' (3) 'I'd suggest + noun/gerund' — 'I'd suggest these throat lozenges.' These are progressively more formal. 'Should' is direct; 'I'd recommend/suggest' softens the advice. Both are widely used in professional health and service settings.

translationVi:
Dược sĩ: Chào buổi chiều. Tôi có thể giúp gì cho bạn hôm nay?
Khách hàng: Xin chào, tôi bị cảm lạnh nặng vài ngày nay và hy vọng bạn có thể gợi ý gì đó giúp tôi.
Dược sĩ: Tất nhiên. Bạn có thể cho tôi biết triệu chứng chính của bạn là gì không?
Khách hàng: Tôi bị chảy nước mũi, đau họng và cảm thấy khá mệt mỏi. Tôi cũng bị sốt nhẹ tối qua, nhưng hôm nay có vẻ tốt hơn rồi.
Dược sĩ: Tôi hiểu. Bạn đã dùng gì cho nó chưa?
Khách hàng: Chỉ dùng paracetamol cho sốt thôi. Tôi chưa thử gì khác.
Dược sĩ: Đó là ý kiến hay. Đối với chảy nước mũi và nghẹt mũi, tôi khuyên dùng thuốc xịt mũi thông mũi. Nó có tác dụng khá nhanh.
Khách hàng: Dùng có an toàn không? Tôi hiện không dùng thuốc nào khác.
Dược sĩ: Có, hoàn toàn an toàn. Bạn nên dùng không quá ba lần mỗi ngày và không quá bảy ngày liên tiếp.
Khách hàng: Được rồi. Còn đau họng thì sao? Buổi sáng khá đau đấy.
Dược sĩ: Tôi gợi ý loại kẹo ngậm trị đau họng này. Chúng có chất gây tê nhẹ giúp làm tê cơn đau. Ngậm một viên mỗi ba đến bốn giờ.
Khách hàng: Hoàn hảo. Có tác dụng phụ nào tôi cần biết không?
Dược sĩ: Đôi khi có thể gây tê nhẹ trong miệng, nhưng đó là bình thường. Chỉ cần không ăn uống trong ba mươi phút sau khi dùng.
Khách hàng: Tốt khi biết điều đó. Tất cả cộng lại là bao nhiêu?
Dược sĩ: Thuốc xịt mũi giá sáu chín chín và kẹo ngậm giá ba bốn chín, tổng cộng là mười bốn tám. Và hãy đảm bảo bạn nghỉ ngơi nhiều — điều đó thực sự có ích.
Khách hàng: Cảm ơn bạn rất nhiều. Tôi sẽ lấy cả hai. Tôi chắc chắn sẽ cố nghỉ ngơi nhiều hơn.

questions.prompt: How long has the customer had her cold?
questions.explanation: 'I've had a bad cold for a couple of days.'

questions.prompt: Which symptom had improved by the time of her visit?
questions.explanation: 'I also had a mild fever last night, but it seems better today.'
questions.hint: She says this symptom 'seems better today'.

questions.prompt: What medication had she already taken?
questions.hint: She took it specifically for the fever.

questions.prompt: What does the pharmacist recommend for the runny nose?
questions.hint: The pharmacist recommends something specifically for the nose.

questions.prompt: How many times a day should the nasal spray be used?
questions.explanation: 'You should use it no more than three times a day.'
questions.hint: The pharmacist gives a maximum number.

questions.prompt: For how many days in a row should the spray be used?
questions.explanation: 'not for more than seven days in a row'
questions.hint: The pharmacist gives a maximum number of consecutive days.

questions.prompt: What does the pharmacist suggest for the sore throat?
How do the throat lozenges work?
questions.explanation: 'They have a mild anaesthetic in them so they help to numb the pain.'

questions.prompt: How often should the lozenges be used?
questions.explanation: 'Suck one every three to four hours.'
questions.hint: The pharmacist gives a specific time interval.

questions.prompt: What should the customer avoid after taking a lozenge?
options: Eating or drinking for thirty minutes
questions.explanation: 'Just don't eat or drink for thirty minutes afterwards.'
questions.hint: The pharmacist gives one specific instruction to follow.

questions.prompt: How much does the nasal spray cost?
questions.hint: The pharmacist gives both prices separately.

questions.prompt: What advice does the pharmacist give besides the medication?
options: See a doctor if not better
questions.explanation: 'make sure you get plenty of rest — it really does help'
questions.hint: He adds one more piece of non-medicine advice at the end.

cloze.template:
Pharmacist: Good afternoon. How can I help you today?
Customer: I've had a bad {{b1}} for a couple of days and I was hoping you could recommend something.
Pharmacist: Can you tell me what your main {{b2}} are?
Customer: I have a runny nose, a sore throat, and I feel quite {{b3}}. I also had a mild {{b4}} last night, but it seems better today.

Customer: Just some {{b5}} for the fever. I haven't tried anything else.

Customer: Is it safe to use?
Pharmacist: Yes. You should use it no more than three times a day and not for more than {{b7}} days in a row.
Customer: And what about my sore throat?
Pharmacist: I'd suggest these throat {{b8}}. They have a mild anaesthetic so they help to numb the {{b9}}. Suck one every three to four hours.
Customer: Are there any side {{b10}} I should know about?
Pharmacist: They can cause a slight numbing of the mouth. Just don't eat or drink for {{b11}} minutes afterwards.

Pharmacist: Ten forty-eight in total. And make sure you get plenty of {{b12}}.

blanks.explanation: 'cold' — the customer says she has had a bad cold.
'symptoms' — symptoms are signs or feelings caused by an illness.
'tired' — she says she feels quite tired as one of her symptoms.
'fever' — she had a mild fever last night which is now better.
'paracetamol' — she took paracetamol to treat the fever.
'nasal' — a nasal spray is used inside the nose.
'seven' — the spray should not be used for more than seven days in a row.
'lozenges' — throat lozenges are medicated sweets you suck.
'pain' — the anaesthetic helps to numb the pain in the throat.
'effects' — side effects are unwanted effects caused by a medicine.
'thirty' — she should not eat or drink for thirty minutes after taking a lozenge.
'rest' — the pharmacist says getting plenty of rest really helps with recovery.

criticalThinkingQuestion: In this conversation, the pharmacist gives advice and sells medicine without the customer needing to see a doctor. In your opinion, what kinds of health problems should be treated by a pharmacist and which ones always need a doctor? Where should the line be drawn?
